title: Synonyms and Shortcuts
subtitle: Equivalent commands you'll see in different vimrcs.
summary: Many Vim commands have multiple keystroke spellings — D = d$, C = c$, S = cc, x = dl, X = dh, etc. Knowing the synonyms helps you read other people's code and your own old macros.

blocks.text: Vim has many *single-key shortcuts* that compress an operator+motion pair. Knowing them helps you type faster and read other people's keystrokes.

blocks.title: The {key:Y} Wart
blocks.text:
Logically {key:Y} should be {key:y}{key:$} (yank to end of line) — that's the pattern set by {key:D} and {key:C}. But for historical reasons {key:Y} is {key:y}{key:y} (yank whole line).

Many users add `nnoremap Y y$` to their vimrc to fix this. Neovim made this the default in 0.6+.

blocks.title: Forty years to fix one keystroke
blocks.text:
{key:D} is the same as {key:d}{key:$}. {key:C} is the same as {key:c}{key:$}. {key:Y} should obviously be {key:y}{key:$}. It isn't — it's {key:y}{key:y}, which already has a perfectly good doubled-form spelling. Why?

The answer is that Bill Joy *left it that way* in 1976. The `vi`-before-`vi` (`ex`) had used capital {key:Y} for yank-line, and rather than break the muscle memory of the (small) existing user base, Joy let the wart stand. Bram Moolenaar inherited Vim's compatibility promise and let it stand too. *Every* vimrc on the planet had `nnoremap Y y$` in it for thirty years, and every Vim user knew to add it. The fix waited for a project willing to break tradition.

That project was Neovim. In 0.6 — November 2021, *forty-five years* after `vi` shipped — the default finally flipped. {key:Y} is the same as {key:y}{key:$} out of the box. Vanilla Vim still ships the old behavior, because Bram's compatibility promise outlived Bram. So now there are two correct answers: in Neovim, {key:Y} is the consistent thing; in Vim, it's still the wart. The number of users who needed those four-and-a-half decades to converge on the obvious behavior is a small monument to how long muscle memory and backwards compatibility can keep the smallest bad decisions in place.
